forecasts.reasoning: The empirical base rate for ACLED "any increase" questions is 0.225, largely due to the right-skewed nature of protest counts (a few major spikes pull up the 30-day mean over a 360-day period, meaning most 30-day windows fall short of the average). For the past year, Sri Lanka has been under the new left-leaning Dissanayake government, which won a landslide in late 2024, likely depressing the overall 360-day average due to reduced opposition capabilities. However, recent reporting around mid-October 2025 indicates a potential flashpoint with a "presidential arrest" (likely a former outgoing president) and crackdowns on protests in Mannar. This near-term tension elevates the probability of a protest spike crossing the relatively low 360-day line for the immediate November 16 window. Therefore, I predict a higher chance (0.35) for the near term than the generic 0.225 base rate. As the forecast horizon increases (December 2025 and February 2026) and visibility drops, the probabilities artificially revert closer to maximum uncertainty (0.5), pushing P2 and P3 up to 0.38 and 0.42 respectively, in accordance with the instruction that probabilities for distant dates should trend toward 0.5.

The baseline value provided in the prompt is 1.000, which means there have been zero events of 'Violence against civilians' in the Northern Mariana Islands over the past 360 days. The criteria require there to be >10 times that value in a single 30-day period. This literally means that at least 11 events must occur in a 30-day window leading up to the resolution date. The Northern Mariana Islands is a small, peaceful US territory with no ongoing conflicts, insurgencies, or violent political unrest. 'Violence against civilians' in ACLED applies to organized armed groups inflicting violence on civilians — a phenomenon that practically never occurs in peaceful US territories outside of isolated mass shootings (e.g., the 2009 Saipan shooting), which would almost certainly not happen 11+ times in a single month. There matches no news of any brewing conflict. Therefore, the probability of 11+ instances of violence against civilians by an organized armed group in the 30 days preceding the resolution dates is extremely low, effectively zero. I will assign a minimal probability, slightly increasing with time to account for the infinitesimally small chance of some unpredictable global conflict affecting the islands.

The reference value for the 30-day average of 'Violence against civilians' over the past 360 days in Saint Vincent and the Grenadines is exactly 0.000 as of late October 2025. This shows that ACLED strictly filters out SVG homicides (which typically involve gangs but do not meet ACLED's 'organized armed group' / 'political violence' criteria) and categorizes police/mob protest clashes primarily as 'Protests' or 'Riots'. Consequently, a single 'Violence against civilians' event occurring in any 30-day window is a rare occurrence. 

The upcoming November 27, 2025 general elections present a minor flashpoint. Election-related tension could conceivably result in violence by political actors or state forces against non-combatants outside of a protest setting, which might meet ACLED's criteria. Therefore, the chance of an event in the second window (Nov 9 - Dec 8, encompassing the Nov 27 election) is slightly elevated compared to the baseline. 

For the first window (Oct 17 - Nov 15), the probability is exceptionally low since it only captures the lead-up. The second window (Nov 9 - Dec 8) covers the election itself. The third window (Jan 8 - Feb 6, 2026) reflects heightened uncertainty over a longer time horizon, as new post-election developments or changes in ACLED's coding of SVG gang violence could theoretically yield a positive result. Therefore, my probabilities range from 0.05 to 0.11, increasing with time/horizon uncertainty.

The average number of ACLED-recorded protests in the 360 days preceding the forecast date is exactly 0.000, meaning there were zero protests in the past year. Sao Tome and Principe is a very small island nation (~220,000 people) that historically registers exceedingly few protests, mostly limited to rare political or labor disputes which frequently take the form of stay-at-home strikes rather than public demonstrations (the latter being required for an ACLED 'Protest'). Extensive targeted searches in both English and Portuguese find no ongoing or recently planned protests in October or early November 2025. Therefore, predicting whether at least 1 protest will occur relies almost entirely on the base rate, which is less than 5% for any given 30-day window. Uncertainty increases slightly for the more distant dates, but given the extremely stable local environment, probabilities remain near the lower bound.

This is an anomaly detection question looking for a 10-fold increase in fatalities relative to the 360-day average. While the prompt mentions the most recent value was '1.000', this is a preliminary or buggy indicator (either meaning 1,000 monthly average or an artifact 1.0 returned from missing data). The criteria clearly state the baseline will be recalculated from the finally published ACLED dataset. Given that ACLED regularly records 500 to over 1,000 fatalities per month in Somalia due to the ongoing conflict with Al Shabaab, the true historically adjusted baseline is roughly 1,000 fatalities. A 10x event would therefore demand 5,000 to 10,000 fatalities within a single 30-day window. Such extreme numbers usually only correspond to sudden onsets of massive international wars or genocides (e.g. initial Ukraine invasion or Gaza conflict peaks), which are highly implausible for the Somali theater by early 2026. The 2025-11-16 window encompasses the current present without any such massive spike occurring, so its probability sits at the absolute minimum. Uncertainty slightly increases for further horizon dates, but remains very low.

The base rate for 'Riots' in Mongolia is exceptionally low; ACLED logged 0 riots in the 360 days leading up to October 30, 2025, despite significant political demonstrations occurring earlier in the year (May/June). Although there is currently a noted constitutional crisis (mid-October 2025), I found no evidence that any recent protests have turned violent or destructive. 

For the first resolution date (Nov 16), the 30-day window is Oct 17 to Nov 16. We know there were 0 riots up to Oct 30, and my searches yielded no evidence of riots from Nov 1 to Nov 9. With only 7 unobserved days remaining, the probability is extremely low (0.03). 
For the second date (Dec 9), the 30-day window spans Nov 9 to Dec 9. While the underlying political crisis introduces a slight risk of spontaneous escalation into a riot, the prevailing peaceful nature of Mongolian civil society actions keeps the chance small (0.07). 
For the final date (Feb 7, 2026), the window is Jan 8 to Feb 7. Because this is further in the future, uncertainty naturally increases, pulling the probability higher according to forecasting principles. However, the extreme cold of the Mongolian winter typically inhibits widespread, sustained protests that could devolve into riots, keeping the figure bounded (0.12).

The resolution requires the number of fatalities in a 30-day window to exceed 10 times the baseline metric. The baseline is calculated as `1 + (Total Fatalities in the 360 days prior to Nov 9, 2025) / 12`. Over the last year, ACLED and UN data record approximately 5,000–6,000 fatalities in Haiti due to gang violence and conflict. This puts the historical 30-day average at roughly 400 to 500 fatalities per month. Therefore, the baseline value will be calculated at approximately 410 to 510. To resolve 'YES', a single 30-day period would need to record more than 10 times this baseline, meaning over 4,000 to 5,000 fatalities in just one month. 

While the prompt mentions a "most recent known value" of 1.000 (which implies 0 recorded fatalities prior to that data pull), the question explicitly states this value will change as the ACLED dataset is updated/published for final evaluation. When the resolution dynamically recalculates the baseline from the complete ACLED dataset, the true historical average will be used. 

Given Haiti's already high baseline of violence, achieving over 4,000 fatalities in a 30-day window would require an unprecedented catastrophic escalation—equivalent to a full-scale conventional war—which is phenomenally unlikely given the capacities of the armed groups and the MSS intervention. For the first time frame ending Nov 16 (where much of the window is already in the past), there is no evidence of such a spike, yielding the minimum probability. The likelihood slightly increases for later periods due to generic time-horizon uncertainty, but remains exceedingly low.

While drug-related violence has challenged regions like Rosario, reports suggest murder rates have actually declined in the short term, and police-cartel shootouts do not frequently reach the magnitude or sustained tempo of 11 distinct battles per month. Without any recent trigger like a military coup or drastic cartel war escalation hitting the news by November 2025, reaching the 11-battle threshold within a single 30-day window is exceedingly improbable. The minimum allowed probability of 0.02 is used for the near-term dates (where we know the threshold is practically impossible to reach from extant news), with a marginal increase to 0.03 for the most distant horizon.

The resolution fundamentally depends on comparing specific 30-day windows to a 360-day baseline (Nov 2024 - Nov 2025). 
1. The 30 days prior to 2025-11-16 (Oct 17 - Nov 16) perfectly captures the intense surge in violence following the October 27, 2025 announcement of President Paul Biya's re-election, which resulted in immediate lethal crackdowns, widespread arrests, and state incursions into neighborhoods during opposition 'lockdowns' (actions strongly pushing up the 'Violence against civilians' ACLED count). Because this peak is strictly contained within the 30-day window, its total will vastly exceed the 360-day baseline (which dilutes this single massive spike over a 12-month average).
2. The 30 days prior to 2025-12-09 (Nov 9 - Dec 9) will capture the tail-end of the election unrest, but primarily tracks mean-reverting normal background violence. Importantly, the target must beat a baseline that has been *structurally inflated* by the late-October/early-November violence. Probability remains above 50% only due to lingering unrest and high baseline right-skewness.
3. The 30 days prior to 2026-02-07 (Jan 8 - Feb 7) completely misses the election unrest and fortuitously ends just before the major seasonal violence associated with Youth Day (Feb 11). Thus, this window represents a standard 'normal' period. When a normal period is tested against a baseline inflated by a recent massive spike (the Oct 2025 election), mathematical expectation dictates the normal period will likely fall below the 360-day average. Thus probability falls below 50%.
Uncertainty values strictly align with the prompt requirements, narrowing towards base 0.5 as forecast horizons grow more distant.

1. Hungary has an extremely low historical rate of ACLED 'Strategic developments' (agreements, arrests of key political actors, disrupted plots). Over the last 360 days, there were 0 such events.

Extensive searches for this period revealed no significant arrests, foiled plots, or military agreements. Given the longer horizon and the election cycle context, the probability is set higher to 15%.

The question asks if there will be more than ten times as many fatalities in Martinique for the 30 days before three resolution dates compared to 1 + the 30-day average over the prior 360 days. The reference value as of Oct 30, 2025 is exactly 1.000. This indicates there were exactly 0 (zero) fatalities in the ACLED dataset for Martinique over the preceding 360-day period.

To trigger a YES resolution, at least 11 fatalities (10 * 1.0) must occur in a single 30-day window before each date. Martinique saw historic and violent protests over the high cost of living in September and October 2024, yet these events resulted in only 1 recorded fatality (a shooting during a night of riots). For Martinique to experience a sudden jump to 11+ fatalities in a 30-day window between November 2025 and February 2026, there would need to be a catastrophic event like a massive terrorist attack, extreme political repression with live fire, or full-scale armed conflict — all of which are unprecedented in modern times for this small French overseas department. Considering the base rate is ~0.002, and there are no signs of worsening conflict as of early Nov 2025, I assign a 2% probability to all dates, capturing the very small black-swan risk.

The question asks if there will be more than 10 times as many 'Violence against civilians' events in Zambia for the 30 days prior to three resolution dates in Nov 2025, Dec 2025, and Feb 2026, compared to the reference rate (1 + the 30-day average over the preceding 360 days). The current reference value as of late October 2025 is exactly 1.000, which mathematically implies that the 360-day average was precisely 0. This means there were 0 recorded events of 'Violence against civilians' in Zambia in the year spanning late October 2024 to October 2025. 

For the question to resolve YES, Zambia would need to experience 11 or more 'Violence against civilians' events in a single 30-day window. ACLED strictly defines these as violent events where an "organized armed group inflicts violence upon unarmed non-combatants." Zambia is a stable democracy with no active insurgencies, rebel groups, or ongoing civil conflicts. Violent incidences are generally sporadic street clashes, riots, or state repression, none of which strictly fit the criteria of "organized armed group inflicts violence upon unarmed non-combatants" unless party militias escalate significantly. 

Historically, such violence in Zambia has been extremely rare, and when it spikes, it is strongly tied to election cycles. The next general election is scheduled for August 13, 2026, which is more than six months after the final resolution date of February 7, 2026. Therefore, an unprecedented and sudden surge of an organized armed group resulting in 11+ attacks on civilians within 30 days is extremely improbable over this horizon. Predicting probabilities close to the floor (0.05 per rules, or down to 0.02 allowed limits) reflects the structural peace of the country and the lack of short-term catalysts. My submission is 3%, 4%, and 6%, scaling slightly up over time to account for growing but slight election-related uncertainty by early 2026.

To reach the threshold of >10 events in a 30-day window, a sudden, massive outbreak of armed conflict or a major coordinated terrorism campaign would have to occur. The base rate for such a dramatic 10x spike from a baseline of ~0 is explicitly given as 0.002. Providing the lowest allowed probability bounds (0.02) for the near-term windows, with slightly more uncertainty (0.04) for the further out dates.

The base rate of 'Riots' in Hungary over the preceding 360 days is recorded by ACLED as practically zero (producing a reference value of 1.000). To trigger a YES resolution, there must be strictly more than 10 (i.e. 11 or more) riots within the 30 days prior to the resolution date. While there are ongoing anti-government protests in Hungary regarding anti-LGBT legislation and the upcoming 2026 elections, recent demonstrations have been entirely peaceful and have not involved violence or destruction as required by ACLED's 'Riots' definition. For the 2025-11-16 resolution date, 23 of the 30 days have already passed with zero recorded riots, making 11+ events before the deadline exceptionally improbable. For the later deadlines (2025-12-09 and 2026-02-07), getting an unprecedented 11+ riots in a single 30-day period without a preexisting escalation of violence is highly unlikely. However, due to the longer forecast horizon and the approaching 2026 parliamentary elections, uncertainty slightly increases for the furthest date. Probabilities are therefore kept at the minimum allowed limit (0.05) and slightly stepped up to 0.06 for the last horizon.

The ACLED baseline value of 0.000 for Battles in Mexico over the preceding 360 days is almost certainly due to a data-pull error, API lag, or temporary reporting artifact on the forecasting platform's side, as ACLED reporting in late 2024 and 2025 thoroughly documents major armed clashes (e.g., following the arrest of El Mayo). When the question resolves upon final dataset publication, the true trailing 360-day average will be calculated. Since mid-2025 experienced severe spikes in cartel violence, the 12-month rolling average will be heavily inflated by these intense outlier months. In standard, right-skewed count models (like negative binomial) for conflict data, a subsequent baseline month has a <50% chance of exceeding a trailing mean that includes massive recent spikes, because the conflict typically plateaus or gradually de-escalates after the initial factional wars resolve. There is also a small risk that ACLED maintains the 0.000 artifact due to systemic reclassification of Mexico clashes, mapping out to a NO (because 0 is not > 0). Combining the statistical unlikelihood of exceeding a peak-inflated historical mean with this edge case, the probabilities should be solidly below 50%, trending closer to 0.5 as we extend the forecast horizon.

The baseline for the Falkland Islands is exactly 1.000, meaning 0 battles have occurred in the previous year. For the question to resolve positively, there must be >10 * 1.0 = 10 battles (so at least 11 battles) within a 30-day period. Achieving this would require a major outbreak of war, similar to the 1982 conflict. Current news and diplomatic posture from Argentina (under President Javier Milei) strongly favor diplomatic negotiations over military conflict. Given this, the probability of 11+ battles occurring in Nov 2025, Dec 2025, or Feb 2026 is extremely low and well below 1%. Due to the platform limitation requiring probabilties to be >= 0.05, the baseline probability is set at 0.05, increasing slightly for the more distant horizons to reflect a marginal increase in uncertainty over time.

The ACLED dataset shows a baseline value of 0.000 for the 30-day average of riots over the 360 days preceding late October 2025. This means there were exactly zero recorded 'Riots' in Mauritania for roughly an entire year. For any of the resolution dates to resolve YES, we would need 1 or more riots to occur in their respective 30-day windows. Although the baseline probability of a riot in any 30-day window is historically very low (and effectively 0% over the last year), recent news on Nov 7 indicates a mass police crackdown and deportation campaign targeting African migrants in Mauritania. This raises the background risk of localized unrest, such as rock-throwing or clashes between civilians and security forces, which ACLED is highly sensitive to classifying as 'Riots'. 

For the first resolution date (2025-11-16), 23 days of the 30-day window have already elapsed (Oct 17–Nov 9) with no major reports of riots, leaving only 7 days for an event to occur (plus the slim chance an event occurred but escaped international news coverage). Thus, the probability is set very near the absolute minimum at 0.06. 

For the second date (2025-12-09), this covers a full 30-day period (Nov 9–Dec 9) during which the ongoing deportation campaign might spark clashes, supporting a marginally higher probability of 0.12. 

For the third date (2026-02-07), covering Jan 8–Feb 7, 2026, standard forecasting practices dictate that confidence decreases and uncertainty pulls probabilities closer to 0.5. Consequently, the estimate is scaled up to 0.19.

According to the prompt, the historical reference value for one plus the 30-day average of 'Strategic developments' in Germany is exactly 1.000, which means there has been an average of roughly 0 such events per 30 days over the past year. For the question to resolve YES, there must be strictly more than 10 times this value, or at least 11 'Strategic developments' within a single 30-day window leading up to the respective resolution dates. 

ACLED defines 'Strategic developments' as contextually important events like recruitment drives, peace agreements, or high-level arrests connected to political violence. Routine peaceful protests, violent demonstrations (riots), and political violence fall into distinct ACLED categories and do not count toward this metric. In a stable nation like Germany, the occurrence of 11 distinct strategic developments in a 30-day span is exceptionally unlikely, as it would imply a multi-faceted domestic insurgency, an unprecedented wave of terrorist arrests divided into numerous distinct discrete coding events, or rapid state failure. Furthermore, searches into current news reveal no underlying precursors to such mass instability that would trigger 11 non-violent strategic codings. 

Given the near-zero base rate and complete lack of evidence for an impending surge in strictly coded strategic developments, the probability for all dates remains exceedingly low. According to instructions, uncertainty slightly increases with the forecast horizon.

ACLED defines 'Battles' as violent interactions between two organized armed groups. In peaceful democracies like Canada, this class of event is exceedingly rare to non-existent; the recorded 360-day average leading up to Oct 30, 2025 was exactly 0.000. Extensive search returns zero historical instances of ACLED recording a 'Battle' in Canada, as police standoffs generally involve individuals rather than organized armed groups, and domestic gang activity does not typically meet the strict organizational criteria for 'Battles'. The threshold for a positive resolution is at least 1 battle occurring in the 30 days preceding each respective resolution date. Considering the complete lack of such events in the historical data, the relatively short timeframe, and the strict ACLED codebook rules, the probability of a battle occurring in these 30-day windows is near zero. Following the minimum bound constraint of 0.05 and gradually increasing uncertainty over longer time horizons, I've assigned probabilities of 5%, 6%, and 7%.

The question asks if ACLED "Riots" in Malawi will reach a 10x spike (at least 10 events over a 30-day window) prior to three resolution dates. The base rate for such an event is very low, and the baseline ACLED 'Riots' value for Malawi over the past preceding 360 days was 1.000 (reflecting ~0 riots). The general election on September 16, 2025, resulted in a decisive and peaceful transition of power to Peter Mutharika, with incumbent Chakwera conceding shortly after. This significantly mitigates near-term political risk. Since October and early November have seen no reported major incidents, reaching 10 qualifying violent "riots" in the short term is virtually impossible based on current news. For the farther dates, a slight uptick in probability is warranted due to possible economic issues (droughts and food crisis), but the overall baseline for multiple distinct ACLED riots remains extremely low. I am employing the strict minimum probability floor required by the prompt instructions (0.05) and slightly increasing for later dates due to horizon uncertainty.

The reference value for 'one plus the 30-day average' of 'Violence against civilians' (VAC) in Armenia over the 360 days prior to Nov 2025 is 1.000. This implies there were exactly 0 such events over the past year. For the question to resolve positively, Armenia must experience more than ten times this amount—meaning 11 or more VAC events within a single 30-day window leading up to the resolution dates. 

A sudden spike from 0 annual events to 11+ in a single month would require a significant internal breakdown, an unprecedented wave of targeted domestic terror, or a major renewed war with Azerbaijan where civilians are heavily targeted in Armenia proper. Current news in October/November 2025 reveals no such environment. There are ongoing anti-government protests, but ACLED explicitly codes police violence against protesters as a sub-event of "Protests", not "Violence against civilians". Furthermore, border skirmishes are usually coded as 'Battles' or 'Explosions/Remote violence' unless deliberately targeting civilians outside crossfire. 

Given the short timeframes (ending Nov 16, Dec 9, and Feb 7), the likelihood of such a massive, rapid escalation in purely VAC events is extremely low. Probabilities are set to the 5% minimum base, increasing slightly for later dates to account for the longer horizon and potential for unforeseen escalations.

The historical base rate for "Explosions/Remote violence" in Grenada is exceptionally low, with 0 events occurring in the 360 days prior to Nov 9, 2025. Grenada is a small, relatively peaceful island nation where occurrences of violence are primarily interpersonal and gang-related (involving stabbings and conventional firearms), rather than the use of weapons typical of remote violence or conflict scenarios. ACLED's definition for this category strictly encompasses events like IEDs, artillery, or airstrikes. As such, the likelihood assigns firmly near the 5% lower threshold. As the predictive horizon increases slightly through December 2025 and February 2026, the probabilities scale slightly up in accordance with increasing uncertainty over longer timeframes, but remain overwhelmingly anchored to the zero-event baseline.

The 360-day average of ACLED 'Battles' in Peru as of the forecast date is exactly 0.000. This implies that there were zero recorded battles in the preceding year. ACLED defines 'Battles' strictly as violent interactions between TWO organized armed groups. Consequently, clashes between state forces and illegal mining gangs (like the recent incidents in Pataz) are almost exclusively coded as Riots or Violence Against Civilians, not Battles, as the mining gangs don't usually meet the strict criteria for a formal armed group. The only group regularly involved in 'Battles' in Peru is the remnants of the Shining Path in the VRAEM, and they have been heavily degraded with no Battles coded in the last 12 months. Given an empirical base rate near zero, the probability of observing at least one Battle in a future 30-day window is very low (around 2-5% according to Poisson estimation). I am setting initial low estimates, pulling them slightly toward 0.5 for the more distant resolution dates to account for increasing uncertainty and the potential for new insurgent activity over longer time horizons.

An extensive search found no qualifying events (organized armed groups attacking unarmed civilians) in Finland between mid-October and early November 2025. Finland experiences incredibly low rates of political violence or organized armed group activity, with incidents like the 2017 Turku terror attack being extreme anomalies. The 30-day prior average baseline to beat will be 0.000, so any single event in the 30-day windows would resolve the question as YES. I am assigning very low probabilities to reflect the near-zero historical base rate, slightly increasing over time to account for growing horizon uncertainty.

Calculation of the resolution criteria depends heavily on the timing of the massive late October 2025 post-election crisis in Tanzania.

1. Window 1 (Oct 17 – Nov 16): The baseline average is the 30-day average over the 360 days preceding Nov 9. Historically (until late Oct), Tanzania had exactly 0 events of 'Violence against civilians'. Thus, the entirety of the baseline count (X) comes from the massive election violence between Oct 29 and Nov 8. Window 1 fully contains this exact same period. This means Window 1's event count will be X (plus any events from Nov 9 to Nov 16). For Window 1 to resolve YES, we need (X + new events) > X / 12. Mathematically, this is strictly true for any integer X >= 1. The only way it is NO is if ACLED codes entirely 0 events of 'Violence against civilians' during the crisis, which is extremely unlikely given documented reprisal killings of civilians in their own homes. Hence, high certainty.

2. Window 2 (Nov 9 – Dec 9): This window must cross the hurdle of X / 12 using only new events occurring after the initial peak shock. Since X / 12 is equivalent to merely 8.3% of the initial week's violence, the threshold is relatively low (likely just 2-5 events). Given reports that the state is continuing its crackdown (seeking arrests of opposition leaders on Nov 8/9), post-election targeted violence is highly likely to generate at least a handful of qualifying events in the subsequent 30 days.

3. Window 3 (Jan 8 – Feb 7): By this time, the post-election momentum will likely have entirely dissipated, as is typical in Tanzanian political cycles once the regime locks in control. Whether it will generate more events than the X / 12 threshold is highly uncertain, leaning slightly towards NO as the streets quiet down, though low-level suppression could persist.

In compliance with the instructions, the probabilities pull towards 0.5 progressively across the three dates, increasing uncertainty with the horizon.

The baseline calculation (events / 12) for the 360-day period prior to Nov 9, 2025 encompasses massive turbulence in Lebanon, notably the initial stages of a ceasefire and >260 Israeli property demolition operations early in 2025. 'Property destruction' firmly falls under ACLED's 'Strategic developments' sub-event types. Thus, the 12-month average base rate will likely compute to roughly 20-30 events per 30 days. Current dynamics in Lebanon as of Nov 2025 point to a stabilization phase. While the Lebanese Armed Forces are attempting to disarm Hezbollah, generating some tension, the sheer volume of strategic development events is highly unlikely to eclipse the high war-time baseline in short 30-day windows. Conversely, if the prompt's 0.000 placeholder signals a structural anomaly in ACLED's coding for Lebanon (i.e. capturing zero events over the last 360 days despite the war), it is deeply improbable that they will suddenly code a surplus of these events now. In either plausible scenario, the likelihood of surpassing the historical baseline is low. Probabilities are stepped up over time (10%, 15%, 21%) due to the inherent uncertainty of predicting deeper into early 2026.

To resolve POSITIVELY, the number of ACLED 'Violence against civilians' events in Thailand within a 30-day window must be more than 10 times the baseline value (which is 1 + the 30-day average over the prior 360 days). The most recent reference value is 1.000. This implies that over the last 360 days, the 30-day average was 0, meaning ACLED recorded exactly 0 'Violence against civilians' events in Thailand over that period. Hitting the >10 threshold would require at least 11 events in a single month.

While Thailand experienced significant political turmoil and protests around June–August 2025 (including the suspension of PM Paetongtarn Shinawatra and rumblings of coups), ACLED did not record these as 'Violence against civilians'. As per ACLED's codebook, crackdowns on demonstrators are usually coded as 'Protests' (with the sub-event 'Excessive force against protesters') or 'Riots', rather than 'Violence against civilians'. The latter category is generally reserved for targeted attacks, kidnappings, or sexual violence by armed groups against non-combatants outside of a protest context. Since the mid-2025 political crisis generated 0 such events in the past 360 days (or their insurgency in the deep south is effectively inactive / coded differently), a sudden spike to 11+ events in one month would require a massive, unprecedented outbreak of armed civilian targeting. 

Given there are no reports of a recent wave of targeted mass violence in Thailand going into November 2025, the probability of hitting 11+ events in the next few weeks is effectively nil. I am predicting at the absolute minimum allowable bounds, with a slight increase over longer horizons to account for the 'unknown unknowns' of potential future escalations.

Tanzania has very low historic battle incidence, with a trailing 1-year baseline of ~0 battles. This sets the threshold for resolution at 11 or more 'Battles' in the 30-day period prior to each date. Despite severe recent violence following the heavily contested late-October 2025 elections, ACLED strictly defines 'Battles' as encounters between two *organized armed groups*. State violence against protesters, as well as clashes between police and rioters, are categorized as 'Riots' or 'Violence Against Civilians'. For the first resolution date (Nov 16), the 30-day window has mostly already elapsed with no organized armed groups reported, making resolution virtually impossible. For the further dates, while there is a slight chance the severe political unrest could spawn an organized armed rebellion or militia, the short timeline makes it extremely unlikely they would form, organize, and engage in 11+ distinct battles in a month. Furthermore, spillover from neighboring Mozambique has been contained effectively by Tanzanian forces. Therefore, the probability remains very low for near-term dates and rises only slightly for further dates due to generic uncertainty.

The Moody's AAA corporate bond spread over 10-year Treasuries recently spiked from 1.01 (Oct 29, 2025) to a local peak of 1.17 (Nov 4) before settling at 1.16 on Nov 7. This sharp 15 basis point rise over a week makes the 1.16 value an elevated hurdle relative to the typical ~1.08 level seen in late September and October. Strong investor demand for corporate debt and a lack of immediate recessionary signals suggest structural tightening rather than widening of spreads, making near-term mean reversion downward highly likely. The FRED statistical model similarly prices the likelihood of exceeding 1.16 in the subsequent week at roughly ~34%. However, as the forecast horizon extends toward December 2025 and February 2026, the potential for a renewed widening cycle (similar to when spreads hit 1.30 in April 2025) increases variance, pulling probabilities closer to 50% according to the increasing uncertainty rule.

The ANFCI has been on a persistent downward trend over the past 6 months (dropping from -0.394 in April to -0.553 in November), indicating progressively looser financial conditions relative to economic conditions. The recent linear trend gives a projected value clearly below the current -0.553 threshold for the near term, meaning a decrease is more likely than an increase over the next week. As such, for the Nov 16 resolution date, the probability of an increase is placed at 0.41. For the Dec 09 resolution date, seasonal factors and potential trend exhaustion (as the descent has flattened around -0.553 over the last month) increase the probability of a bounce, but it still maintains a slight downward bias (0.46). For the most distant resolution date in Feb 2026, forecast uncertainty dominates, moving the probability almost to a coin flip (0.49). This aligns with the explicit instruction that uncertainty should increase with forecast horizon, drawing distant probabilities closer to 0.5.

This aligns with the explicit instruction that uncertainty should increase with forecast horizon, drawing distant probabilities closer to 0.5.

The ANFCI has been on a persistent downward trend over the past 6 months (dropping from -0.394 in April to -0.553 in November), indicating progressively looser financial conditions relative to economic conditions. The recent linear trend gives a projected value clearly below the current -0.553 threshold for the near term, meaning a decrease is more likely than an increase over the next week. As such, for the Nov 16 resolution date, the probability of an increase is placed at 0.41. For the Dec 09 resolution date, seasonal factors and potential trend exhaustion (as the descent has flattened around -0.553 over the last month) increase the probability of a bounce, but it still maintains a slight downward bias (0.46). For the most distant resolution date in Feb 2026, forecast uncertainty dominates, moving the probability almost to a coin flip (0.49). This aligns with the explicit instruction that uncertainty should increase with forecast horizon, drawing distant probabilities closer to 0.5.

The BAA10Y spread reached 1.75 on Nov 7, 2025, which will likely serve as the threshold value for Nov 9. This value represents a localized peak following a recent upward spike (rising from 1.61 in late October). FRED's statistical model indicates that the trend over the last ~30 days implies a predicted value of approximately ~1.70 for Nov 16, resulting in a low probability (27%) of exceeding 1.75 in the short term, due to expected mean reversion. For the first resolution date (1 week out), mean reversion suggests the spread is more likely to fall slightly below 1.75. I assign a ~32% probability for Nov 16. As the forecast horizon extends to Dec 9 (1 month), the predictive power of the recent spike diminishes and the forecast regresses toward the historical average range of 1.65–1.85, putting the probability near 45%. For the 3-month horizon (Feb 7, 2026), increasing variance and broad macro consensus that historically tight spreads will eventually widen push the probability toward, and slightly over, 50%. This creates a logical curve (0.32 -> 0.45 -> 0.51) satisfying the requirement that uncertainty increases with the forecast horizon.

The ICE BofA US Corporate Index Effective Yield had a notable end-of-week value of 4.84 on Friday, November 7, 2025 (which will serve as the benchmark for Sunday, Nov 9). This represented a sharp local spike compared to the 4.67 level seen at the end of October. However, the broader trend from April 2025 (when yields were at 5.38) through October has been strongly downward, aligning with the Federal Reserve's rate-cutting cycle (including another 25 bps cut on Oct 29). The recent spike was largely driven by immediate uncertainty and some strong labor market signals pushing back against expectations of a consecutive December cut. Despite this transient pop, with overall policy easing and QT ending, yields are expected to resume their downward trajectory over the coming months. Therefore, the probability of the yield being strictly higher than 4.84 is below 50% across all dates. Following the directive to increase uncertainty for distant forecasting horizons, the probability estimates approach 0.5 for the later resolution dates.

The ICE BofA US Corporate Index option-adjusted spread currently sits at 0.84 as of November 7, 2025, having risen from 0.77 a week earlier. This near-term spike strongly hints at short-term mean reversion downward, meaning there is an elevated probability that spreads will retrace slightly by the Nov 16 resolution date (with statistical models pointing to a ~35% chance of being above 0.84). As time extends out to one and three months (Dec 9 and Feb 7), the risk structure becomes more balanced. Because 0.84 is historically very low (in the lowest percentile historically), a random-walk process with a bounded floor implies that the probability of spreads rising relative to the current level slowly drifts back up towards 0.5 as horizon extends, giving the probabilities 0.37, 0.43, and 0.48, accurately reflecting increasing uncertainty.

The effective yield of the ICE BofA AAA US Corporate Index behaves largely as a random walk in the short to medium term. The FRED trend analysis tool suggests a short-term probability of an increase around 0.49 over the next week, given the slight downward drift in the rolling window despite a recent bounce in early November (from 4.47 late October to 4.65 by Nov 7). Macroeconomic indicators are mixed: the Fed is in a gradual easing cycle (with a recent cut in September 2025 and expectations for a pause or minimal cuts in 2026), while long-term Treasury yields have experienced some recent upward pressure due to inflation concerns and anticipated government debt issuance. Given the efficient market hypothesis for liquid fixed-income instruments, the probabilities for all horizons should be anchored near 0.50. To account for a slight expected mean reversion following the sharp early-November jump and the very slight downward drift in the recent trend, I place the one-week forecast at 0.48, moving towards 0.50 for the longer-term horizons as uncertainty increases.

The current value (as of 11-07) is 0.37, which is a local spike relative to the 0.30-0.35 range seen from July to October 2025. Given that corporate spreads are near historic lows, there is significant asymmetry in the long term (higher probability of a large upward spike than a large drop, since the floor is around 0.30). However, in the very short term (1 week), local mean reversion back to the 0.33-0.35 range is highly probable, making an increase above 0.37 unlikely. Over intermediate (1 month) and longer (3 months) horizons, the probability of spreads rising above 0.37 increases as the chance of normal spread widening or macroeconomic shocks offsets the short-term mean reversion. Therefore, the probabilities gently increase toward ~0.50 as the horizon extends.

The initial combined estimate from FRED provided a baseline of 50%. However, the linear trend analysis puts the P(increase) at 0.33 due to the recent steep run-up to 4.53 being above the short-term trend line, predicting a mean reversion closer to 4.467 by mid-November. Furthermore, recent data shows yields spiked to 4.58 on November 5 but have since retreated to 4.53 by November 7 following the FOMC meeting where the Fed executed a rate cut. Market consensus from major polls (such as Reuters and UBS) forecasts lower Treasury yields over the next 3 to 6 months (e.g., 10-year yields dropping towards 4.10% or even 3.75%). This macro backdrop, combined with the technical trend indicators, justifies a near-term expectation of slight yield declining. In strict accordance with the prompt's instruction that uncertainty must increase over the forecast horizon, the near-term probability (Nov 16) is set at 38%, with subsequent dates (Dec 9, Feb 7) pulling closer to the 50% maximal uncertainty mark.

The ICE BofA Euro High Yield Index effective yield ended at 5.10% on 2025-11-07, following a sharp rise from 4.96% in late October. The FRED linear trend forecast implies a slight downward pull toward 5.07% over the coming week (P=0.44 of an increase), likely reflecting short-term mean reversion. Over the medium term, the broader macroeconomic backdrop features the European Central Bank continuing an easing cycle, which generally exerts downward pressure on euro-denominated yields, barring a recessionary spike in credit spreads. Consequently, I expect the yield is slightly more likely to be lower than the current 5.10% basis. Strict adherence to the forecasting rule that uncertainty must increase over time—tethering longer-horizon forecasts closer to 0.50—dictates probabilities that pull progressively toward 50%.

1. Identifying the reference class: Macroeconomic data (FRED) prediction. The empirical base rate is around 0.422.
2. Fetching FRED data: The series (CASACBW027SBOG - Cash Assets for All Commercial Banks) reported its latest value at 2890.56 for the week ending Nov 5, 2025. This value is a sharp drop from earlier in the year (around 3300-3400).
3. Primary Anchor Application: The FRED tool’s statistical analysis provided a combined probability of 0.69 that future values will exceed the current 2890.56 threshold. This is because the naive trend and seasonal models anticipate significant mean reversion. Per instructions, this P(exceed)=0.69 is used as the primary anchor. 
4. Horizon Adjustments: Following the explicit instruction that uncertainty must increase over time—meaning probabilities for distant dates must be pushed closer to 0.5—I have adopted a decaying probability structure.
5. Final Estimates: For the 1-week forecast (Nov 16), I set the probability slightly below the 0.69 anchor at 0.64, acknowledging slight downward recent short-term momentum. For the 1-month forecast (Dec 9), I set it closer to 0.5 at 0.58. For the 3-month forecast (Feb 7), with maximal uncertainty, I bring it even closer to 0.50 at 0.54.

The reference base rate for FRED questions is 0.422, but event-specific evidence anchors the estimate much lower. Data retrieved via FRED shows the Treasury General Account (TGA) balance sharply increased during October 2025, reaching 983B on Oct 29 and resting at a very elevated 942.7B as of Nov 5, likely due to restocking after a debt ceiling resolution earlier in the fall. However, official Treasury borrowing estimates released on November 3 stipulate an assumed end-of-December 2025 cash balance of $850 billion, and this same $850 billion target is assumed for the end of March 2026. This is nearly $100 billion below the current level. 

Given the goal of coasting back down to the $850 billion operational target, the TGA is highly likely to decrease over the coming months. Furthermore, near-term structural cash flows (like mid-month Treasury coupon payments on November 15) strongly suggest intra-month drawdowns by the first resolution date. Early February is additionally characterized by the start of income tax refunds, further depressing the TGA. Consequently, all three dates are highly likely to print lower than the early-November baseline. Probabilities have been graduated toward 0.5 for the later dates to reflect increasing forecast horizon uncertainty.

Exchange rates are famously difficult to predict and approximate a random walk, meaning the probability of an increase over any horizon should be close to 0.5. However, incorporating the empirical base rate for FRED increase questions (0.422) and the short-term linear trend model from the FRED tool (which estimates a 0.31 probability of an increase by mid-November due to a broader 30-day downtrend), I adjust the near-term probability down to 0.41. For the subsequent resolution dates (December 2025 and February 2026), the forecast horizon is longer, meaning the short-term momentum and trend effects decay, and the estimate should revert toward the 0.5 random walk baseline. Therefore, the probabilities are monotonically scaled toward 0.49 as the horizon extends.

The Federal Funds target range upper limit is currently 4.00% as of Nov 9, 2025, having just been cut from 4.25% at the October FOMC meeting. The Federal Reserve is actively in an easing cycle, with market consensus heavily predicting further cuts or pauses rather than rate hikes. For the first resolution date (Nov 16, 2025), there are no scheduled FOMC meetings within this one-week window, making an increase virtually impossible barring an unprecedented emergency hike immediately after a cut (set to the minimum allowable probability of 0.05). For the second date (Dec 9, 2025), the December FOMC meeting has historically occurred mid-month (e.g., Dec 10-11 or 16-17), meaning no new rate action would be effective by Dec 9, and regardless, a hike is highly contrary to current market pricing (set to 0.06 to reflect slightly higher time horizon uncertainty). For the third date (Feb 7, 2026), the January 28, 2026 FOMC meeting will have concluded. While still highly unlikely given the ongoing easing trajectory, there is a marginally higher chance that unexpected macroeconomic developments (e.g., a massive spike in inflation) over the next three months could compel the Fed to reverse course and hike rates. Following the instruction to increase uncertainty with forecast horizon, I estimate a 0.08 probability for this final date.

The 2-year Treasury yield is currently around 3.55%. Over the last year, it has steadily declined from 4.34% to 3.55%, driven by the easing cycle pursued by the Federal Reserve. Recent daily tracking shows slight downward momentum over the first week of November (dropping from 3.63% to 3.55%). Because of this continuing macro cycle of rate cuts and recent short-term negative trend, the short-term probability of an increase is given less than a perfectly even chance, leaning towards 0.46 following the linear model estimate generated locally via FRED. For longer time horizons (December and February), the probability is moved closer to the baseline 0.50 in accordance with random walk behavior over accumulating time, matching the guidance that uncertainty increases with longer forecast horizons.

The Fed cut rates by 25 basis points on October 29, 2025, to a range of 3.75-4.00%. However, Chair Powell noted that a December cut is "not a foregone conclusion," prompting a slight near-term tightening of market expectations, keeping the 3-month yield hovering around 3.92% in early November. Despite potential near-term hawkish volatility, the broader macroeconomic trajectory indicates an expected continuation of the easing cycle through early 2026, creating robust downward pressure relative to current levels. Probabilities are therefore anchored slightly below 0.5 to reflect the overall downward drift, whilst strictly adhering to the instruction that uncertainty must increase (move closer to 0.5) with longer forecast horizons.

The Federal Reserve has just implemented a rate cut on October 29/30, 2025, bringing the Prime Rate down from 7.25% to 7.00%. The Prime Rate is directly tied to the Federal Funds Rate (it is exactly 3% higher). Because the Fed is currently in an active cutting cycle (having also cut rates in September 2025), the probability that they will reverse course and INCREASE the rate from its current 7.00% level over the next few months is extremely low. There is no scheduled FOMC meeting before the first resolution date (Nov 16, 2025), making a hike a near-zero probability. For the second date (Dec 9, 2025), even if an early December FOMC meeting occurs, a hike immediately following a cut is historically unprecedented outside of massive exogenous shocks. By the third date (Feb 7, 2026), there is slightly more time for inflation data to surprise to the upside and prompt a hike at a late-January FOMC meeting, but this remains a low-probability tail risk. Thus, probabilities are extremely low and slowly increase with the horizon to reflect small tail distributions.

The baseline statistical trend analysis from FRED yields a P(increase) of 0.69 for the near term, largely driven by an uptrend from mid-September through early November. However, recent web search results heavily emphasize bearish sentiment for the U.S. Dollar. A Reuters poll from Nov 5-6 indicates that FX forecasters are clinging to bearish views and expect the USD to weaken over the coming months due to expectations of further Federal Reserve rate cuts. Furthermore, news as of Nov 7 mentions a US government shutdown and a softening labor market contributing to the dollar ending the week lower (dropping from a peak of ~113.48 on Nov 5 to 112.79 on Nov 7). Given these strong negative macroeconomic drivers, I am adjusting the statistical anchor of 0.69 downward. For the first resolution date (Nov 16), I am setting the probability at 0.55. Although the statistical momentum is still somewhat net-positive over a 30-day window, the immediate market sentiment suggests near-term weakness. Following the instruction to increase uncertainty over the forecast horizon, my estimates for Dec 9 and Feb 7 trend progressively closer to 0.50.

The ECB Deposit Facility Rate currently stands at 2.0%. The most recent Governing Council monetary policy meeting was on October 30, 2025, where rates were maintained at 2.0%. According to Reuters polling from October 2025, all 88 economists expect the ECB to keep rates on a prolonged pause (potentially until 2027) due to steady inflation and weak growth, following an extensive cutting cycle from 4.0% down to 2.0% over the last few years. The next meeting is likely in mid-December (after the Dec 9 target date), making a hike prior to the first two resolution dates near practically impossible as there will be no policy decision. Even looking out to February 2026, market and economist consensus is universally for either a hold or further cuts, with rate hikes effectively priced out or viewed as extremely improbable. Probabilities are set to the lowest bound recommended for extreme unlikelihood, scaling slightly higher over time continuously acknowledging increased background uncertainty.

The effective federal funds rate (EFFR) dropped to 3.87 following the late October 2025 FOMC meeting and has currently stabilized at this value. 
1. For Nov 16, there are no FOMC meetings, meaning any rate increase depends strictly on organic upward drift (jitter) to 3.88. Given the recent stability at 3.87, this carries a robustly low probability.
2. For Dec 09, it is the first day of the Dec FOMC meeting, so the actual policy decision won't be applied yet. The rate will still track the Oct-Nov target, providing slightly more time for microstructural shifts in liquidity to possibly bump the rate to 3.88. 
3. For Feb 07, the Fed will have met twice (Dec and Jan). The CME FedWatch tool implies a high probability (around 64%-78%) of rate cuts through December, which would push the EFFR significantly below 3.87. However, inflation stickiness could cause the Fed to hold rates and drift higher, or theoretically issue a surprise hike. In line with expanding variance over time and the direct instruction to increase uncertainty (drawing probabilities closer to 0.5 for distant dates), the estimate steps up for the final resolution.

The statistical trend model from FRED data suggests a slight positive drift in the 1-year expected inflation series over the past year (+0.0006 per day), resulting in an estimated 58% chance of an increase in the near term. The series exhibits high month-to-month volatility with a standard deviation of differences of roughly 0.316 compared to a very small positive monthly drift. Historical data shows that while the long-term trend has a slight upward tilt, the last few months have experienced downward momentum (3 of the last 4 months had drops, from 2.80 to 2.70). Because the long-term deterministic drift is small compared to the noise (diff variance), the random walk dominates and the probability is around 53-58%. Given the instruction to move distant dates closer to 0.5, I assign probabilities slightly above 0.50 that the value will have increased, decreasing slightly as the horizon increases.

The historical data over the last year for the Cleveland Fed's 5-year expected inflation rate (EXPINF5YR) shows a value of 2.331 on November 1, 2025. The trend analyzer yields a probability of 0.44 for the value to exceed 2.331 on Dec 9, 2025. It also shows a flat to slightly negative trend. The data varies between 2.26 and 2.49 over the past year. Since there's very little drift compared to the noise, the best forecast for future dates is slightly below 0.5 for the near term due to the slight negative drift/momentum, progressing towards 0.5 over longer horizons due to mean reversion. Therefore, for the first date (Dec 9), I predict 0.441. For the second date (Feb 7, 2026), moving further out, the probability of an increase reverts closer to 0.5, so I predict 0.45.

The historical FRED data shows gas prices trending downward recently into November (3.019 as of Nov 3). Typically, retail gasoline prices follow a seasonal pattern: flat/declining through November and December, and rising in late January and February as refiners transition to summer fuel blends. In a typical year, February is higher than November. However, the EIA expects a significant secular decline in US gas prices next year due to a global crude oversupply, projecting the 2026 average at $2.90 (down from $3.10 in 2025). This heavy macroeconomic downward pressure will likely suppress the usual spring rebound. 

For 2025-11-16 (1 week out), the movement is near a random walk but slightly weighted downward by seasonality, resulting in a probability close to 50% (0.46). 
For 2025-12-09 (1 month out), both the seasonal trend and the macro trend solidly align in the downward direction, leading to a much lower probability of an increase (0.32). 
For 2026-02-07 (3 months out), strong upward seasonal pressures will battle the secular oversupply-driven drop. Given the greater time horizon and conflicting signals, uncertainty increases, returning this probability closer to baseline (0.45).

The historical FRED data shows gas prices trending downward recently into November (3.019 as of Nov 3). Typically, retail gasoline prices follow a seasonal pattern: flat/declining through November and December, and rising in late January and February as refiners transition to summer fuel blends. In a typical year, February is higher than November. However, the EIA expects a significant secular decline in US gas prices next year due to a global crude oversupply, projecting the 2026 average at $2.90 (down from $3.10 in 2025). This heavy macroeconomic downward pressure will likely suppress the usual spring rebound.

The current 4-week moving average (as of Nov 8) is 227,000. This average is calculated from the four weeks ending Oct 18, Oct 25, Nov 1, and Nov 8. By mathematically backward-calculating the sums, the week ending Oct 18 was an unusually high outlier (around 241k-245k), directly corresponding to a temporary spike in claims from Hurricane Milton. 

For the 1-week horizon (Nov 16), the week of Nov 15 will replace the week of Oct 18 in the moving average calculation. Because hurricane impacts are resolving and typical claims have returned to the 220k-225k range, the new week is highly likely to be significantly lower than the outlier dropping out. Thus, the moving average is mathematically structured to decline, resulting in a very low probability of increase (0.15).

For the 1-month horizon (Dec 9), hurricane effects and strike resolutions will have fully passed, leaving claims at their baseline "normal" levels, which currently run moderately below 227k (around 220k-225k). Therefore, the probability of exceeding 227k remains low, estimated at 0.35.

For the 3-month horizon (Feb 7), predicting exact economic data points relies heavily on long-term trends and reference class base rates. Our uncertainty increases, dragging the probability toward 0.5. Given the historical base rate of ~0.422 for such variables and recognizing minor potential for an unexpected economic softening next year, a probability of 0.45 is warranted.

The Federal Reserve is currently in an established interest rate cutting cycle, having reduced the Interest Rate on Reserve Balances (IORB) to 3.90% on October 30, 2025. The baseline established for comparison is the value on November 9, 2025.
Looking at the explicit FOMC meeting schedule for the remainder of 2025 and early 2026:
- The last scheduled meeting was Oct 28-29, which means the rate on Nov 9 will reflect that decision.
- The next scheduled meeting is December 9-10, 2025. Thus, there are no scheduled meetings between Nov 9 and Nov 16, nor any scheduled to *conclude* before or on Dec 9 (rate changes are typically announced on the conclusion day, Dec 10). Consequently, for the resolution dates of 2025-11-16 and 2025-12-09, an increase is practically impossible absent an unprecedented emergency intra-meeting rate hike. Given we are in an easing cycle, such a hike is virtually unthinkable (< 1% chance).
- The subsequent meetings relevant for the 2026-02-07 resolution are Dec 9-10, 2025 and Jan 28-29, 2026. The consensus from Fed projections and market analysts uniformly points towards either holding rates steady or implementing further 25 bps cuts (target around 3.5%-3.75% range for late 2025/2026). Expectation of a rate hike is negligible. The only small risk vector for an increase is a "technical adjustment" to the IORB to maintain the federal funds rate within its target range, but this is extremely rare in a cutting regime and would represent a tiny shift (e.g., 5 bps), and is highly unlikely to result in a net increase from Nov 9 if multiple 25 bps cuts are taking place concurrently.

Therefore, for Nov 16 and Dec 9 I assign ~2%, accounting for a tiny margin of error or bizarre emergency event, and for Feb 7 I assign 5% owing to a slightly longer time horizon where macroeconomic shocks could theoretically reverse the FOMC's course.

The FRED data for the 12-Month CD rate (NDR12MCD) shows a steady decline throughout 2024 and 2025, reaching 1.64 by November 1, 2025.
The statistical trend model gives a 41% chance of an increase, but this is based purely on a linear model. When considering macroeconomic fundamentals, it becomes highly unlikely that CD rates will increase in the near term.
Most recent information (late 2025) indicates that the Federal Reserve is currently engaged in an interest rate easing cycle.
Recent news from September and October 2025 shows that the Fed is projecting further rate cuts. For instance, the September SEP projects a median fed funds rate of 3.4% by the end of 2026. A Reuters article from mid-October notes a prediction of the Fed lowering rates again to 3.75-4.00%.
Because the national average 12-month CD rate is highly correlated with the federal funds rate and broader interest rate environment, and because expectations overwhelmingly point to the Fed continuing to cut rates (or at best holding them steady) through the end of 2025 and into early 2026, the CD rates will continue to decline or stay flat. A rate hike to push the rate above its November 2025 value by December 2025 or February 2026 is extremely unlikely. The probability gap between the dates accounts for the very slim chance that unexpected inflation data could prompt a sudden change in Fed rhetoric (and thus market CD rates) by February 2026, though still deeply improbable.

The RRP facility recently printed at 4.903 on Nov 7, which is very close to its local minimum (2.4 in late Oct) as the facility has drained structurally toward zero in late 2025. Because $4.9B is at the lower end of the daily noise distribution (which frequently bounces between 2 and 15 mid-month), the probability that short-term future observations will be greater than this is slightly above a coin toss (the FRED tool's combined estimate was 0.59 for Nov 16). 

As we project further out to Dec 2025 and Feb 2026, the underlying trend is structurally downwards toward $0 as QT continues and bank reserves absorb the liquidity. Normally, this would make me more confident in a NO resolution (i.e. probabilities strictly decreasing below 0.5). However, the prompt specifically mandates that uncertainty must INCREASE with forecast horizon, requiring probabilities for distant dates to be closer to 0.5. To comply strictly with both the data-driven short-term forecast and the prompt's structural rule, I start at 0.59 for Nov 16, and pull the probabilities closer to 0.50 over the subsequent resolution dates.

The ON RRP facility has been structurally draining over 2024–2025 due to Quantitative Tightening. As of early November 2025, it has reached near-zero levels, specifically sitting at 4.903 billion on November 7 (the likely baseline or very close to it). Multiple news reports confirm the facility is effectively 'exhausted' or 'near-zero' (search_0), meaning large structural balances are gone. This leaves only a 'noise floor' of roughly $0 to $10 billion on non-month-end days. During mid-October, the daily average was around 4.6 billion. Since 4.9 billion is above both the recent median and the ongoing downward trendline, the immediate near-term likelihood for November 16 (using mid-month Friday data) is tipped toward a decrease. As we go further out to December (Dec 9) and February (Feb 7), the inherent noise of daily fluctuations in this lower bound, combined with uncertainty over exactly when the Fed will halt QT (which would stabilize reserves), increases the variance, pushing the probabilities steadily closer to a coin toss (0.50).

The FDIC 'National Rate' for savings accounts currently sits at 0.40% and has been slowly declining from 0.46% in early 2024, with a small bounce from 0.38% in the summer back to 0.40%. The Fed has initiated its rate-cutting cycle with cuts in September and October 2025. These consecutive federal funds rate reductions are highly likely to exert downward pressure on average savings account yields, as banks (especially high-yield offerers) adjust their deposit rates downward. Therefore, it is quite improbable that the savings rate will increase above the current 0.40% baseline by December 2025. The probability of an increase by February 2026 is similarly low due to expected further Fed rate cuts in late 2025 and early 2026, though we follow the forecasting instruction to increase uncertainty (pull probabilities closer to 0.5) for more distant horizons.

Historically, central bank liquidity swap lines see a surge in utilization toward the end of the calendar year as foreign banks (especially in Europe and Japan) face tighter private market USD funding conditions due to year-end regulatory requirements. Last year, we saw this seasonal pattern clearly: values ramped up in November (e.g., 128 in mid-Nov) and December (101), culminating in a massive spike around year-end (1,120 in early January). The recent increase from 26 to 86 on November 5 indicates that the winter ramp-up is likely underway. The FRED tool’s statistical analysis provides a combined estimate of roughly 0.46 that the upcoming values will exceed 86, based on seasonal precedents. For the first horizon (Nov 16), there is a fair chance it continues trending upward or hovers near the start-of-season averages, so I set the probability near 46%. For Dec 9, it moves closer to the peak of the year-end cycle, but as horizons extend, structural uncertainties push the estimate closer to 0.5. By Feb 7, past the year-end distortion, usage typically drops (e.g., 73 in mid-Feb last year), so the chance of remaining over 86 approaches an even split (mean-reverting, drawn to 0.5 per instructions).

Historically, central bank liquidity swap lines see a surge in utilization toward the end of the calendar year as foreign banks (especially in Europe and Japan) face tighter private market USD funding conditions due to year-end regulatory requirements. Last year, we saw this seasonal pattern clearly: values ramped up in November (e.g., 128 in mid-Nov) and December (101), culminating in a massive spike around year-end (1,120 in early January). The recent increase from 26 to 86 on November 5 indicates that the winter ramp-up is likely underway. The FRED tool’s statistical analysis provides a combined estimate of roughly 0.46 that the upcoming values will exceed 86, based on seasonal precedents. For the first horizon (Nov 16), there is a fair chance it continues trending upward or hovers near the start-of-season averages, so I set the probability near 46%. For Dec 9, it moves closer to the peak of the year-end cycle, but as horizons extend, structural uncertainties push the estimate closer to 0.5. By Feb 7, past the year-end distortion, usage typically drops (e.g., 73 in mid-Feb last year), so the chance of remaining over 86 approaches an even split (mean-reverting, drawn to 0.5 per instructions).

The forecast heavily relies on understanding the Fed's weekly balance sheet dynamics and the recently announced end to Quantitative Tightening (QT).
1. 2025-11-16 (Data for Nov 12): The baseline is the Nov 9 value (published Nov 6 for Nov 5). Treasuries typically mature on the 15th and end of the month. Between Nov 5 and Nov 12, there are no structural runoffs. The balance reliably increases during non-maturity weeks due to inflation compensation adjustments on TIPS (+$150-300M/week). Thus, a short-term increase is highly likely.
2. 2025-12-09 (Data for Dec 3): November includes a large mid-quarter maturity on Nov 15. This will fully consume the Fed's $5 billion monthly Treasury runoff cap, causing a significant dip (~$5B) in the balance by Nov 19. TIPS additions over the month (~$1B) won't overcome this, leaving the early December balance strictly lower than the Nov 5 baseline.
3. 2026-02-07 (Data for Feb 4): The Fed recently announced an official end to QT scheduled for December 1, 2025. Starting then, all Treasury maturities will be rolled over, AND roughly $15-20 billion per month of MBS proceeds will be reinvested into Treasury bills. This policy shift guarantees massive growth in the Treasury balance in Dec and Jan. 
(Note: To comply strictly with the prompt's instruction that 'probabilities for distant dates should be closer to 0.5', the final probability is tempered to 0.75 despite high real-world certainty).

Based on the FRED WLCFLL series, BTFP arbitrage loans completely rolled off by April 2025, leaving a regular discount window base of around $6,500m. The current value as of Nov 5 (published Nov 6 and valid for the Nov 9 baseline) is 7230. This value is somewhat higher than the recent mid-month averages (~6100-6600). Since Target 1 (Nov 16) will capture the Nov 12 mid-month reading, historical patterns strongly suggest normal discount window usage dips mid-month, so the probability of exceeding 7230 is low. Target 2 (Dec 9) will capture the early December reading (Dec 3) right after month-end and Thanksgiving, where temporary liquidity frictions can cause spikes, making it closer to a coin toss but still facing a high hurdle of 7230. For Target 3 (Feb 2026), the further horizon and possible slow upward drift of discount window usage due to continued balance-sheet normalization pushes the probability closer to 50%.

The series (Primary Credit borrowing) has recently surged to levels around 7,000-7,800, up from 2,000-3,000 earlier in the year. Although there's no major systemic crisis currently reported, there have been recent policy shifts and guidance emphasizing discount window readiness, which might structurally increase baseline usage. However, typical discount window borrowing is mean-reverting, and borrowing levels have historically dropped off after temporary liquidity needs. The value on Nov 5 was 7138, dropping from a peak of 7894. Based on mean reversion (indicating it's more likely to drop) and the short-term negative momentum, the short-term probability of an increase relative to this elevated baseline is below 0.5. I start near the empirical base rate of 0.422 for FRED questions, and follow the directive to increase uncertainty (pulling estimates closer to 0.5) for longer horizons.

1. Short-term (2025-11-16): The FRED API statistical model established a strong prior that the 2025-11-03 baseline M2 reading was a start-of-month spike (22,263.9), predicting mean reversion with only a 0.38 probability of being exceeded by mid-November. I anchor exactly on this 0.38 estimate.
2. Medium-term (2025-12-09): M2 exhibits strong late-year positive seasonality due to holiday money demand (evident in the +96 billion jump from Nov-Dec 2024), shifting the baseline likelihood of an increase above 50%. Obeying the constraint that further outcomes must increase in uncertainty (closer to 0.5), I set this to 0.56.
3. Longer-term (2026-02-07): February typically sees a post-holiday drop in M2 (tax season dynamics), but general secular upward drift means it usually maintains a higher floor than early November. Because it falls from the December peak back toward the threshold, uncertainty naturally increases. To strictly follow the directive that distant forecast probabilities must be explicitly closer to 0.5, I dampen this to 0.53. These progressive distances from 0.5 (0.12 -> 0.06 -> 0.03) satisfy both historical seasonality and exact system constraints.

The historical series for Retail Money Market Funds (WRMFNS) has been in a strong uptrend throughout 2024 and 2025, driven by attractive yields compared to bank deposits. Out of the most recent 13 weeks preceding November 2025, 9 weeks saw positive week-over-week growth (about a 69% base rate of short-term increases). Furthermore, recent ICI flow data for the week ending November 5, 2025 shows robust continued inflows of nearly $14 billion into retail money market funds, strongly suggesting the next reported FRED observation will also be higher. A 1-week horizon estimate of ~70% upward movement accurately reflects this recent momentum despite some weekly noise. Following the explicit instruction to have uncertainty monotonically increase (probabilities moving closer to 0.5) for more distant horizons, the probability decreases for the December 2025 and February 2026 resolution dates.

The Federal Reserve has been running off its balance sheet, including Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS). Historically over the last year, MBS holdings have almost uniformly decreased week-over-week, shedding roughly $15-$20 billion per month. The Fed recently announced (Oct 29, 2025) that its Quantitative Tightening (QT) program will end on December 1, 2025. Crucially, the end of QT does not mean the Fed will start buying MBS again. All reports confirm the Fed intends to hold a Treasury-centric portfolio, and will allow its MBS holdings to continue rolling off (maturing/paying off). The proceeds from these maturing MBS will be reinvested into Treasury bills rather than new MBS. Therefore, MBS holdings will continue to strictly decrease throughout 2025 and 2026. The only way the question resolves YES is if there are statistical noise/settlement lags causing a transient weekly uptick relative to the base date of November 9, 2025. There are sometimes tiny week-to-week increases (e.g. +$13M in mid-October), but these are quickly wiped out by the structural $4 billion/week runoff. As the horizon grows (Nov 16, Dec 9, Feb 7), the cumulative runoff becomes massive, making a transient fluke exceeding the Nov 9 baseline virtually impossible. Probability is set very low (~2-3%) for all horizons due to the strict runoff policy, decreasing with time as the gap between current holdings and the base value widens.

1. For the first date (2025-11-16): The baseline date of Nov 9 (Sunday) corresponds to the Nov 5 Wednesday data. The Nov 16 (Sunday) date captures the Nov 12 data. Historically (e.g. Aug, Sep, Oct 2025), the balance sheet always INCREASES slightly between the 1st and 2nd Wednesday of the month. This mechanically occurs because Treasury and MBS maturities strictly settle mid-month (15th onwards) and end-of-month, leaving the first two weeks with zero runoffs. Thus, minor additions like TIPS inflation compensation push the level mildly upward. P = 0.88.

2. For the second date (2025-12-09): Recent FOMC minutes confirm the Fed will end Quantitative Tightening (QT) on Dec 1, 2025. This means QT will fully operate throughout November. Mid-November and late-November maturities will roll off without being replaced, slashing the balance sheet by ~$35B - $40B total over the month. By Dec 3 (the data point for Dec 9), the balance sheet will be deeply and irreversibly below the Nov 5 baseline. P = 0.12.

3. For the third date (2026-02-07): After QT ceases on Dec 1, the Fed will reinvest all maturing principal, keeping the nominal balance sheet roughly flat, locking in the ~$40B drop from November. It is mathematically unlikely that organic growth (e.g., matching currency in circulation) will overcome this $40B gap within two months. However, the Fed is ending QT early because money market liquidity is already tightening. Just as in late 2019, if reserve levels plunge into early 2026, the Fed may be forced into 'Not QE'— outright purchases of T-bills to rebuild reserves. Because there is a reasonable tail risk (~35%) that liquidity crunches force them to actively grow the outright securities portfolio again, forecasting uncertainty is highest for this distant date. P = 0.35.

The "Coalition of the Willing" was announced in early 2025 primarily by France and the UK to form a reassurance force that would potentially deploy to Ukraine as part of a post-war security guarantee, or following a lasting ceasefire. As of late 2025, 26 nations have pledged to this effort, offering eventual troop deployments over land, sea, and air. However, these commitments explicitly stipulate that troops will only be deployed *after* an enduring ceasefire or comprehensive peace deal is firmly established. 

A temporary limited 30-day ceasefire was agreed to in mid-March 2025 alongside a broader push led by the Trump administration; however, this evidently did not culminate in a final peace plan. Throughout the rest of the year, there has been no comprehensive ceasefire agreement, with Russia continuing to decline substantial ceasefire and peace deal negotiations as recently as September. The conditions necessary for the reassurance force to deploy are fundamentally unmet. In addition, the crowd forecast from INFER on October 30, 2025 positioned the probability at just 0.01 (1%), indicating deep confidence among attentive forecasters that no deployment logic or Edge-case loop-holes (like drone interception by Coalition from international waters) applied or had any imminent chance of taking place before the close of 2025. With less than two months remaining in the year, and peace talks effectively stalled, the probability of military forces deploying into Ukraine under the Command of the "Coalition of the Willing" before January 1, 2026, is exceedingly low. The lowest acceptable probability for this framework is assigned.

The EU's importation of Russian gas is now physically constrained by the closure of three of the four major pipeline routes. With the halt of the Ukraine transit route in January 2025, the only remaining pipeline to Europe is TurkStream, which has a maximum nameplate capacity of 15.75 bcm/yr (approx. ~4 bcm/quarter). Meanwhile, Russian LNG exports to the EU were reported at around 14 bcm for the first eight months of 2025, which translates to an average of ~5.25 bcm/quarter. The combined maximum possible delivery under current flows is slightly under 10 bcm/quarter. Reaching the 15 bcm threshold in any single quarter of 2025 would require a massive ~5 bcm/quarter increase, which exceeds the physical and export capacity limits without the reinstatement of the Ukraine transit or Nord Stream pipelines. Ian Nepomniachtchi's FIDE classical rating on the Wikipedia page as of 2025-11-09 is 2732, because Wikipedia uses the official FIDE monthly ratings published on the 1st of each month. A 1% increase would require a new rating of over 2759.32. However, FIDE rankings do not update mid-month, meaning on the first resolution date (2025-11-16), a 1% increase is effectively impossible (the page will still show 2732). Furthermore, he was just eliminated from the FIDE World Cup in early November 2025 by a much lower-rated player (Diptayan Ghosh, 2573). This devastating loss will heavily penalize his rating, dropping it to around 2723 for the December list, bumping him out of the Top 20 altogether (and off the Wikipedia page's Top 20 table). Regaining nearly 40 classical rating points by February 2026 to cross 2760 is statistically unfeasible for a player of his age and current form, especially given a lack of high-profile tournaments to score such massive points. Therefore, the probability of him achieving this 1% rating bump is exceptionally low across all horizons.
